Province: Bougainville (AROB), East New Britain, Milne Bay
Specific Location: Outer island margins, Solomon Sea corridor, Baining Mountains
Impact & Casualties: 50+ deaths. The strongest recorded cyclone in the region's history. Triggered destructive landslides in East New Britain (killing 12) and severe coastal flooding that left over 114,000 people in need of humanitarian aid.
670 to 2,000+ estimated deaths. A massive, early-morning mountainside collapse buried over 150 homes under 8 meters of rock and mud, entirely cutting off the Highlands Highway.

Specific Location: Epicenter in Hela; widespread throughout the Highlands
Impact & Casualties: 160+ deaths. Shaking triggered hundreds of massive landslides that severed major infrastructure, dammed rivers, and destroyed rural villages, affecting half a million people.
25+ deaths. A massive limestone hillside failure near an LNG quarry swept down and completely buried a village under a 10-meter wall of debris.
150 deaths. Torrential rains caused catastrophic flash flooding. Major rivers burst their banks, washing away bridges and completely isolating the province for weeks.

Hundreds of indirect deaths. Severe drought dried up rivers, while extreme night frosts wiped out staple sweet potato (kaukau) crops, leaving 40% of the population facing severe food shortages and malnutrition.

Man-Made. Following a tailings dam failure, roughly 90 million tons of mine waste were discharged into the river annually. It contaminated 1,300 km2 of land, poisoning forests, destroying fisheries, and ruining the livelihoods of 50,000 people.

Province: Western Highlands, Jiwaka, Chimbu, Eastern Highlands
Specific Location: Wahgi Valley and Highlands Highway corridor
Impact & Casualties: 9,355+ displaced and over 50,000 affected. Days of torrential rain triggered simultaneous flash floods and landslides across four Highlands provinces, severing the Highlands Highway and destroying food gardens.

Thousands affected. A magnitude 6.9 quake struck amid severe Sepik River flooding; the Yuat and Karawari tributaries burst their banks, submerging food gardens, aid posts and houses across all five Angoram LLGs.

53,000+ displaced nationwide. A surge of king tides flooded coastal villages and low-lying atolls - up to 30,000 affected in New Ireland and coastal/atoll communities of ~50,000 in Manus - inundating homes, gardens, water catchments and cemeteries.
50,000+ affected. Unusually high king tides flooded tens of coastal and downstream estuary villages, inundating staple breadfruit and sago crops and contaminating freshwater sources.
5 deaths. Heavy rain triggered a landslide that flooded the Vibo River and swept away a family of alluvial miners - three adults and two children - at their mining site.
13+ deaths (15 feared). A pre-dawn landslide buried an alluvial gold-mining camp between rugged mountains accessible only by helicopter, burying miners asleep in their tents.
